text:
ByKate Wieczorek,

Contributor.

Forthe first time in Olympic history, the 2024 Paris Olympics showcase an equal split of male and female global athletes. Team USA has sent 314 women and 278 men, a 53/47 percent split. With the passing ofEqual Pay for Team USA Act in 2022, gender equality is at the forefront of the American Olympic team. However, this has not translated to U.S. jobs, even in predominately female industries.

Healthcare workers and related technical occupations possess the altruistic and difficult mission of keeping us healthy and well. Even though women represent almost 50 percentage points more in the industry, men make nearly double the average salary of women in this field.





Source:Data USA

Additional education could necessitate a higher salary, however, this is not the case. The number of women achieving educational degrees in healthcare greatly outnumber the number of men, regardless of the type of degree awarded (Figure 1).



According to theAmerican Association of University Women, at the current rate of progress it will take until 2088 to close the gender pay gap. The marketing industry is also contributing to unequal pay.Despite women occupying 61% of the industry, men collect nearly an extra $32,000 when comparing average salaries.





Source:Data USA

Marketing analysts have been primarily female for over 10 years. Since 2014, women have held at least a 14 percentage point lead above men (Figure 2) yet continue to be underpaid.



Despite women holding nearly 80% of these jobs, elementary and middle school male teachers make nearly 13% more than their female cohorts. Furthermore, early education female teachers work on average 1.5 hours more per week, according toresearch published in Frontiers in Psychology.





Source:Data USA

Female elementary and middle school teachers not only paid less than their male counterparts, but also fall $7,350 short ofthe average salary the U.S. workers. This is particularly debilitating considering that teachers work more hours per week than other working adults, according toa 2023 American Teacher Survey

Every business needs Human Resource (HR) Managers. The U.S. Bureau of Labor anticipate a 5% job growth for HR Managers between 2022 – 2032, higher than the projected rate for all occupations. While women account for 70% of Human Resource Managers, they earn nearly 15% less than their male coworkers.





Source:Data USA

As organizations continue to focus on attracting and retaining top talent, effective Human Resource management isessential to a company’s success. Businesses need to reassess how they are compensating female leaders in HR.

This is not an exhaustive list of every female-dominated industry that is slacking in paying women. Overall, the U.S. gender pay gap is stifling and stagnant – with women earning around80 cents for every dollar men earnedfor the past two decades. The 2024 Olympics gives us hope that with systemic changes and continued advocacy, we can move the needle forward toward closing the gender pay gap.
title: Unlike The 2024 Olympics, Female-Dominated Industries Undervalue Women
author: Kate Wieczorek
description: With more U.S. women athletes and the Equal Pay for Team USA Act, gender equality is at the forefront of the Olympics. However, this has not translated to U.S. jobs.
keywords: Olympics,leadership,leadership strategy,pay gap,gender pay gap,salary,Women in the workplace
